question: What first drew you to Marketing, and how has that interest evolved since?
lookingFor: A genuine, evolving story grounded in a specific observation or experience — evidence of real intellectual curiosity about how brands, messages, and consumer behaviour interact, not just 'I like advertising'.

bullets: Name a specific trigger — a campaign that made you stop and think, a family business, a school enterprise activity, or a piece of wider reading
Show how curiosity developed over time: reading, work experience, an experiment you ran yourself
Land on a specific aspect of marketing that now genuinely interests you intellectually
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: It started when I noticed that two nearly identical energy drinks sat on the same shelf and one outsold the other by miles — the only real difference I could see was the packaging and sponsorship. That mystery pulled me into thinking about brand perception, and I started reading around it: first Rory Sutherland's Alchemy, which reframed advertising as applied psychology rather than persuasion, and then I ran a small experiment for my school's charity fair, changing only the name of a product and testing whether sales shifted. They did, noticeably. Since then I've become genuinely obsessed with why people buy things they can't fully explain rationally, and that question is what I want to spend three years exploring properly.

commonMistakes: 'I like social media and creativity' with no intellectual depth underneath
Motivation framed purely around getting a job in advertising
A story with no development — the interest sparked once and never grew
followUps: Which aspect of marketing — consumer behaviour, digital channels, brand strategy, market research — interests you most right now, and why?

question: Why do you want to study Marketing at this university specifically?
lookingFor: Specific, researched reasons tied to this department — evidence that you have gone beyond the prospectus and understand what makes this course distinctive.

bullets: Name something concrete: a module, a live brief with an industry partner, a research centre, or a faculty member's published work
Connect it to a specific interest or experience of your own
Show you understand what differentiates this course from similar programmes elsewhere
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Your course caught my attention because of the live campaign module in the second year, where student teams work with real brands on actual briefs rather than simulated cases. That distinction matters to me because I've already seen in a small school project how different real constraints are from hypothetical ones — a client's actual sign-off changes every decision you make. I also read Dr Hassan's work on behavioural nudges in retail environments, which connects directly to the shelf-perception question I've been thinking about since I started reading Sutherland. I didn't want a programme that front-loads theory and leaves application to placement year; I wanted one that weaves both together from the start.

commonMistakes: Praising the university's general reputation or rankings with nothing specific underneath
Describing modules that belong to a different institution's course
Motivation so generic it would apply to any marketing department
followUps: What would you bring to the cohort that other candidates might not?

question: Tell us about a marketing book, article, podcast, or case study you've engaged with recently that isn't part of your school syllabus.
lookingFor: Genuine intellectual curiosity beyond the classroom — the ability to engage critically with an idea rather than simply summarise it.

bullets: Name a specific source and give enough detail to show you actually read or listened to it
Identify one idea you found genuinely interesting, surprising, or contested
Relate it to a question you're now thinking about
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've been reading Byron Sharp's How Brands Grow, which challenged a lot of assumptions I'd taken for granted. His argument that brand loyalty is largely a myth and that reach matters far more than targeting contradicts almost everything I see practised in social media marketing. What I found most interesting was his evidence on mental and physical availability — that brands grow by being easy to recall and easy to find, not by building emotional relationships with a niche. I'm not sure I fully agree, because there are categories where loyalty clearly does exist, but it made me want to look at market share data through a completely different lens.

commonMistakes: Naming a source without engaging with any of its actual ideas
Choosing something from the school curriculum and calling it wider reading
Summarising without any critical perspective
followUps: Do you agree with the author's central argument? Where would you push back?

question: Why don't consumers always make rational purchasing decisions, and why does that matter for marketers?
lookingFor: A grasp of behavioural economics applied to marketing — the candidate should show they understand that emotion, heuristics, and context shape buying decisions, and can say what that means practically.

bullets: Name at least one specific cognitive bias or heuristic (anchoring, social proof, loss aversion, etc.) with a brief explanation
Give a real or plausible example of how it affects a purchase
Explain the marketing implication — how understanding irrationality lets you design better interventions
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Consumers rely heavily on mental shortcuts because evaluating every purchase rationally would be exhausting. One I keep coming back to is anchoring — if a product is listed at £200 marked down to £120, people perceive value relative to the original price even if the £200 was arbitrary. Another is social proof: we use others' choices as a signal of quality, which is why review counts often matter more than review averages. For marketers, this means the context and framing around a product can be as powerful as the product itself. If you design a pricing page or a shelf position without understanding these effects, you're leaving decisions to chance.

commonMistakes: Vague statements about 'emotions' without naming any specific mechanism
No concrete example to ground the idea
Treating irrationality as a flaw to correct rather than a reality to design around
followUps: Can you think of a marketing campaign that has exploited one of these biases well or badly?

question: What is the difference between a brand and a product, and why does it matter?
lookingFor: A clear conceptual distinction — the candidate should articulate that a product is a physical or digital offering while a brand is the set of associations and meaning that surrounds it, and explain the commercial significance.

bullets: Define product (what is made or sold) and brand (what people feel, associate, and believe about it)
Illustrate with a concrete example — ideally one where the brand premium is visible in the price or market share
Explain why a strong brand gives competitive advantages that a product alone cannot
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: A product is the physical or functional thing being sold — the coffee beans, the trainers, the software. A brand is the accumulated meaning around it: the story, the associations, the trust, the feeling someone gets when they see the logo. Apple's laptops and a similarly specced competitor's laptop can be compared on processing speed, but you can't compare the meaning of owning one. That difference in meaning is exactly what allows Apple to charge a premium and why people queue for products before they've seen them. A product can be copied quickly; a brand, built over years, is genuinely hard to replicate — which is why it's one of the most durable sources of competitive advantage.

commonMistakes: Describing a brand as just a logo or visual identity
No commercial implication — treating the distinction as purely semantic
Giving an example where the brand and product distinction isn't actually visible
followUps: Can a brand ever become a liability? Give an example.

question: Can you explain the marketing mix and walk us through how one company you know well uses it?
lookingFor: Solid grounding in the 4Ps (Product, Price, Place, Promotion), and the ability to apply the framework to a real example rather than recite a textbook definition.

bullets: Correctly define all four elements without getting them muddled
Apply them to a real company with enough specificity to be convincing
Show that the elements interact — a decision in one affects the others
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The marketing mix is the set of controllable variables a company uses to meet its target market's needs. The classic four are Product — what's being sold and its features; Price — the pricing strategy; Place — how and where it reaches the customer; and Promotion — how it communicates. Looking at Oatly: the Product is oat milk positioned on sustainability and taste; the Price sits in the premium tier, above store-brand plant milks but below specialist alternatives, which reinforces its quality signal; the Place spans supermarkets but also premium coffee shops, which shaped early perception before retail expansion; and the Promotion is deliberately weird and self-aware, which distinguishes it in a category full of earnest health claims. What I find interesting is that their place strategy — getting into indie coffee shops first — was actually doing brand-building work, not just distribution.

commonMistakes: Reciting the four Ps correctly but failing to apply them
Using a vague, oversimplified company example
Treating the four elements as independent rather than interdependent
followUps: What would change in the mix if Oatly decided to compete on price rather than premium positioning?

question: What is market segmentation, and what are the risks of getting it wrong?
lookingFor: A clear explanation of segmentation and targeting, plus genuine critical thinking about what can go wrong — over-segmentation, stereotyping, or missing an unserved group.

bullets: Define segmentation accurately: dividing a market into groups with shared characteristics
Name at least two segmentation bases (demographic, psychographic, behavioural, geographic)
Identify a real risk with a plausible example
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Segmentation is dividing a broad market into smaller groups whose members share meaningful characteristics, so you can target each group with a more relevant offer. You can segment by demographics like age or income, by behaviour such as purchase frequency, by psychographics like values and lifestyle, or by geography. The risks cut in both directions. If you over-segment, you fragment your reach and spend heavily targeting slivers of the market — you might also stereotype groups inaccurately, which alienates the people you thought you were reaching. If you under-segment, your message is so broad it resonates with no one in particular. A real failure mode I find interesting is when brands segment by age and design campaigns that treat all forty-year-olds as identical — ignoring that behavioural and psychographic data within that group varies enormously.

commonMistakes: Confusing segmentation with targeting — they are sequential steps
Listing segmentation types without explaining why you'd choose one over another
No discussion of the risks or failure modes
followUps: How would you decide which segmentation basis to use for a new product launch?

question: What do you see as the most significant difference between digital and traditional marketing, and is one superior to the other?
lookingFor: A balanced, evidence-grounded view — the candidate should resist a simplistic 'digital is better' answer and show understanding of what each format genuinely does well.

bullets: Identify a meaningful structural difference: measurability, interactivity, reach, cost, or targeting precision
Acknowledge what traditional formats still do that digital cannot replicate as well
Land on an integrated view rather than declaring a winner
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The most significant difference, in my view, is measurability. Digital marketing lets you track a customer from the first impression through to purchase and attribute revenue to a specific ad or email. Traditional media — a billboard, a TV spot — builds awareness and brand salience in ways that are genuinely hard to trace. That doesn't make it weaker; it makes it different. There's strong evidence that high-reach broadcast advertising is still one of the most effective ways to build mental availability over time, which is Byron Sharp's argument. For most brands the right answer is integration: digital does precision and conversion; traditional does scale and cultural presence. Declaring one superior ignores that they answer different questions in the purchase journey.

commonMistakes: Stating digital is superior simply because it's measurable without acknowledging what's lost
No examples or evidence — just assertions
Ignoring the role of context and category — what works for a fashion brand may not work for a utility company
followUps: Can you think of a campaign that successfully combined both, and what made the combination work?

question: Marketing has been criticised for manipulating consumers and creating false needs. How would you respond to that charge?
lookingFor: Mature engagement with a genuine critique — the candidate should acknowledge the legitimate concerns while also making a considered case for ethical marketing's role in society, not simply defend the industry reflexively.

bullets: Acknowledge that the critique has real substance — some advertising is manipulative, some does exploit vulnerable groups
Distinguish between exploitative marketing and marketing that genuinely informs or creates value
Show you've thought about where the boundaries should be
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The critique is partly right, and I think being honest about that is important. There are advertising practices — targeting children with junk food, using fear to sell things people don't need, dark patterns in digital interfaces — that are genuinely manipulative and harmful. I don't want to defend those. But the broader charge, that marketing as a discipline creates false needs, is harder to sustain. Marketing often reveals needs people couldn't articulate — the iPhone didn't create the need for connectivity, it found a shape for a need that already existed. I think the ethical line sits around honesty: does this communication help someone make a better decision for themselves, or does it exploit a cognitive bias to override their judgment? Regulation matters here too — standards bodies and advertising codes exist because the industry can't always self-regulate.

commonMistakes: Defending marketing reflexively without acknowledging any legitimate concern
Agreeing that all marketing is manipulation with no nuance
No engagement with where you'd personally draw the ethical line
followUps: What's a specific advertising practice you think should be banned or more tightly regulated?

question: Tell us about a marketing campaign you genuinely admire and explain what makes it effective.
lookingFor: The ability to analyse a real campaign with genuine rigour — not just 'it was creative' but a structured account of why it worked commercially and communicatively.

bullets: Name the campaign and give enough context for the panel to follow
Explain the strategic insight behind it, not just the execution
Say what it achieved — brand perception, sales, cultural reach — and why that followed from the approach
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've been thinking about Dove's Real Beauty campaign since reading about it for a project. What I admire isn't the creative execution alone — it's the insight underneath it. Dove identified that the beauty industry's own advertising was making women feel worse about themselves, and they built a campaign that directly contested that norm. The strategic move was to make the market's standard practice into the villain, which differentiated Dove without attacking a named competitor. It worked because the insight was genuinely true and resonant: research backed it up and the audience recognised themselves in it immediately. The long-run risk is that the gap between campaign values and company ownership can become visible, which did happen with Unilever's other brands, but as a piece of strategic thinking it's still impressive.

commonMistakes: Describing what the campaign looked like without explaining why it worked
Choosing a campaign purely because it went viral with no strategic analysis
No acknowledgment of any limitation or risk in the approach
followUps: What would you have done differently about that campaign?

question: What do you think makes social media marketing effective for some brands and ineffective for others?
lookingFor: A critical, evidence-grounded view on social media — not a recitation of platform features but genuine thinking about fit between brand, audience, and format.

bullets: Identify at least two factors that determine effectiveness — audience fit, content format, brand voice, category relevance
Use a concrete brand example where social media does and doesn't fit
Show that you understand the difference between engagement metrics and business outcomes
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I think the biggest determinant is whether the category is naturally social. Fashion, food, and fitness belong in people's social feeds because people already share those topics without any brand prompting. When a brand shows up there with content that fits what audiences are already doing, it can be invisible in a good way. But a B2B software company forcing itself onto TikTok often fails because the category doesn't naturally translate — the content feels grafted on. The second factor is brand voice: brands with a distinctive, consistent voice can build a following; brands that mimic trending formats without a real personality just produce noise. And there's a measurement trap — high engagement can mean low sales impact if the audience engaging isn't the buyer. Virality and commercial effectiveness are often different things.

commonMistakes: Treating follower count or likes as measures of marketing success
Assuming every brand should be on every platform
No mention of the gap between engagement and commercial outcome
followUps: Which social platform would you choose for launching a new sustainable sportswear brand, and why?

question: How has data changed marketing, and what limits does data still have?
lookingFor: A balanced view — the candidate should show they understand the genuine power of data-driven marketing while also recognising its blind spots: correlation without causation, the gap between stated and revealed preferences, privacy constraints.

bullets: Name at least two concrete ways data has improved marketing decisions
Acknowledge at least one genuine limitation — measurement gap, privacy, causal inference, or data quality
Show you've thought about what data cannot tell you
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Data has made marketing far more accountable. You can run an A/B test on two subject lines and know within hours which performs better, or segment your audience by actual purchase behaviour rather than assumed demographics. That kind of precision has moved marketing closer to a discipline of evidence rather than intuition. But the limits are real. Attribution models tell you what someone clicked, not why they bought — they measure correlation in a customer journey but rarely causation. Survey data has the opposite problem: people will tell you what sounds good rather than what they actually do. And as privacy regulation tightens, the data marketers have relied on — third-party cookies, device fingerprinting — is shrinking. I think the best marketing teams are ones that use data to sharpen decisions without treating a dashboard as a substitute for understanding the customer.

commonMistakes: Treating data as infallible — no awareness of its limitations
Vague references to 'big data' without any concrete example of what it enables
No engagement with privacy or ethical constraints on data use
followUps: What's one question a marketer regularly wants to answer that data still can't answer reliably?

question: Why is pricing considered a marketing decision and not just a finance decision?
lookingFor: Understanding that price communicates meaning as well as recouping cost — it shapes brand perception, signals quality, and interacts with consumer psychology in ways that make it inseparable from marketing strategy.

bullets: Make the key point: price signals quality and shapes perceived value, not just margin
Give at least one example where price works as a brand signal
Acknowledge the interaction with the rest of the marketing mix
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Price is a marketing decision because it communicates meaning before anyone has used the product. A luxury perfume priced at £30 would signal something entirely different about quality and exclusivity than the same liquid priced at £250 — the price is part of the brand. Giffen goods and Veblen goods show that in some categories demand actually rises with price because price itself is the signal consumers are buying. Beyond perception, pricing interacts with everything else: a premium price requires premium distribution and a premium communication tone; a penetration price requires volume and changes what you can spend on product quality. Finance asks whether the price covers costs and generates margin; marketing asks what the price means to the customer and how it positions you against alternatives. You need both, but the marketing question is often the more consequential one.

commonMistakes: Treating price as purely a cost-plus calculation
No example of price working as a brand or quality signal
Missing the interaction between price and the rest of the mix
followUps: Can you think of a brand that prices counter-intuitively and explain why it works?

question: How does marketing to businesses differ from marketing to individual consumers?
lookingFor: A clear grasp of the structural differences — decision-making unit, buying cycle length, rational versus emotional weighting, relationship importance — and an ability to explain why these differences change the marketing approach.

bullets: Identify the decision-making unit in B2B — multiple stakeholders, longer cycles, more formal evaluation
Contrast with the more individual, often faster, more emotionally influenced B2C decision
Show how these differences change the marketing mix in practice
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: In B2C, you're usually addressing one person or a small household who makes a relatively quick decision shaped by habit, emotion, and social influence. In B2B, you're typically dealing with a buying committee — a finance director, a procurement team, a technical evaluator — who operate through a formal process that can take months and involves risk assessment, procurement policies, and post-sale support as part of the evaluation. This changes almost everything: B2B content tends to be detailed and educational because buyers need to justify the decision internally; B2C advertising prioritises instant recognition and emotional resonance because you're competing for attention in a scroll. That said, there's a growing body of evidence that B2B buyers are still human and that brand and emotional trust matter more than pure rational evaluation — which means the distinction is real but often overstated in the 'B2B is all logic' direction.

commonMistakes: Saying B2B is purely rational and B2C is purely emotional without nuance
No mention of the decision-making unit or buying cycle
Treating the two as completely different disciplines with no common principles
followUps: Which do you personally find more interesting to study and why?

question: What is greenwashing, and what responsibility do marketers have to avoid it?
lookingFor: A clear definition of greenwashing, a real example, and a substantive discussion of why it's harmful and what good environmental marketing looks like instead.

bullets: Define greenwashing precisely: environmental claims that are misleading, unsubstantiated, or disproportionate to actual practice
Give a real or plausible example
Articulate what responsible environmental marketing looks like and who is accountable
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Greenwashing is when a company makes environmental claims in its marketing that are misleading, unverifiable, or wildly disproportionate to its actual impact. A classic example is a fast-fashion brand launching a small 'sustainable collection' while its core model remains high-volume, low-durability production — the collection gets the headlines but doesn't change the environmental footprint. Marketers are responsible because they're the ones choosing what claims to foreground and how to frame them; the compliance team can approve copy technically, but the marketer decides what story to tell. The harm is real: greenwashing misleads consumers making genuine efforts to buy sustainably, rewards bad actors, and erodes trust when it's exposed. Good environmental marketing is specific and verifiable — a brand saying 'this product uses 40% recycled material, and here's how we're measuring it' is very different from 'we care about the planet'.

commonMistakes: Defining greenwashing too vaguely — any bad environmental claim
No concrete example to ground the definition
Treating it purely as a legal or compliance issue with no discussion of the marketer's role
followUps: Can you think of a brand you believe is doing environmental communication honestly? What does it do differently?

question: What do you think is driving the growth of influencer marketing, and what are its limitations?
lookingFor: A grounded analysis of why influencer marketing has grown and a balanced, critical view of where it falls short — authenticity risks, measurement challenges, and audience fatigue.

bullets: Explain at least two reasons for growth: trust erosion in traditional advertising, direct community relationships, targeting precision
Name at least two genuine limitations: disclosure issues, fake engagement, brand safety, attribution difficulty
Show you've thought about where it fits versus where it doesn't
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The growth is driven partly by declining trust in traditional advertising — people have become very good at ignoring banner ads and television spots, but they haven't become as good at filtering recommendations from someone they actively follow. Influencers also offer something very precise: a pre-built audience that brands can access with relatively low creative production cost. The limitations are significant though. Fake engagement and follower inflation mean brands can pay for an audience that doesn't exist. Disclosure requirements are widely flouted, which erodes the authenticity that made the format valuable in the first place. And attribution is genuinely hard — if someone buys a product a week after seeing an influencer mention it, which touchpoint gets the credit? There's also a cultural saturation problem: audiences are getting better at recognising sponsored content and adjusting their trust accordingly.

commonMistakes: Treating influencer marketing as an unambiguous success story
No engagement with the measurement or authenticity problems
Framing it purely in terms of follower counts without discussing engagement quality
followUps: Would you advise a challenger brand to use influencer marketing as its primary channel? Why or why not?

question: What is the difference between qualitative and quantitative market research, and when would you use each?
lookingFor: A clear conceptual distinction and practical judgement about which method suits which question — not a rote recitation of definitions.

bullets: Define each type accurately: quantitative measures what and how many; qualitative explores why and how
Give an example use case for each
Show that they are complementary rather than competing
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Quantitative research gives you numbers — survey responses, sales data, A/B test results — and tells you what is happening and how often. Qualitative research gives you depth — interviews, focus groups, ethnography — and helps you understand why it's happening and what it means to the people involved. If I wanted to know whether customers prefer red or blue packaging, a survey of a thousand people is the right tool. If I wanted to understand why one packaging option makes people feel more premium than another, I'd need to sit with people and hear them explain it in their own words. The real power is in sequencing them: qualitative research helps you form the right hypotheses; quantitative research lets you test them at scale. Running one without considering the other often means you get either numbers without explanation or explanation without generalisability.

commonMistakes: Saying qualitative is subjective and therefore less reliable without acknowledging its unique value
No practical example of when you'd choose one over the other
Treating them as alternatives rather than complementary tools
followUps: What method would you use to test whether a new product name is working, and how would you design the study?

question: Some researchers argue that true brand loyalty barely exists — that what looks like loyalty is really just habit or lack of alternatives. How would you evaluate that claim?
lookingFor: Genuine engagement with a contested academic argument — the candidate should show they've encountered this debate, can evaluate the evidence on both sides, and can form a nuanced view rather than simply agreeing or disagreeing.

bullets: Show familiarity with the argument — ideally Byron Sharp or switching cost research
Acknowledge the evidence on the sceptical side: most buyers are light and polygamous across brands
Make a credible case for where real loyalty does seem to exist — and under what conditions
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The claim draws heavily from Byron Sharp's work, and the data behind it is genuinely robust: in most fast-moving consumer goods categories, the majority of a brand's sales come from light buyers who also buy competitors, not from a loyal core. What looks like loyalty in these categories is largely availability and habit — you buy the same cereal because it's at eye level and you're not thinking hard about it. I find that persuasive for low-involvement categories. But there are categories where something more durable seems to happen: Apple users who actively resist switching, sports fans buying club merchandise at a premium, people who genuinely trust one pharmacist over another. I suspect the distinction is involvement and identity: when a brand becomes part of how someone sees themselves, switching has a psychological cost that isn't present when you're choosing between two similar cereals. So I'd say the sceptical view is broadly right about most purchases, and commonly overstated when applied to high-involvement categories.

commonMistakes: Defending brand loyalty uncritically without engaging with the empirical challenge
Agreeing entirely that loyalty doesn't exist without acknowledging where it clearly does
No engagement with any specific evidence or researcher
followUps: Does your answer have implications for how much a brand should invest in loyalty programmes?

question: Have you done anything outside the classroom that has helped you understand marketing better — work experience, a project, running something, or experimenting with something yourself?
lookingFor: Real evidence of initiative beyond the syllabus — the candidate should describe a specific experience, what they did, what they observed, and what it taught them that they couldn't have learned from a textbook.

bullets: Name a specific activity: running an event, managing a school's social account, helping a family business, a personal project
Describe what you actually did, not just the title of your role
Identify one concrete thing it taught you about marketing that surprised or challenged you
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I ran the social media account for our school's charity fundraiser for about six months. I thought the hard part would be making the content look good, but the actual challenge was figuring out why some posts got shares and others got nothing — there was no obvious pattern at first. I started testing systematically: same photo, different captions; different posting times; content about the cause versus content about the people involved. What surprised me most was that posts that made someone specific visible — a name, a face, a single person's story — consistently outperformed aggregate impact posts even though I'd assumed donors wanted to know the scale of the difference they were making. It made me read more about identifiable victim effect, which I didn't realise had a name until I looked it up.

commonMistakes: Describing a role in vague terms — 'I helped with marketing' — with no specifics
No concrete observation or learning from the experience
Treating the experience as box-ticking rather than as a source of genuine curiosity
followUps: What would you do differently if you ran that project again?

question: Why can a marketing campaign that works brilliantly in one country fail in another, even for the same product?
lookingFor: Understanding of cultural context, meaning, and localisation — the candidate should show that marketing meaning is culturally constructed and that global brands face a genuine localisation challenge.

bullets: Identify the core reason: meanings, humour, values, and aesthetics vary across cultures
Give at least one real or plausible example of a cross-cultural failure or necessary adaptation
Show you understand the tension between global brand consistency and local relevance
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The core problem is that meaning is culturally constructed. A colour, a gesture, a piece of humour, a family archetype — these carry very different weight and connotation depending on the audience's cultural context. The classic example is Pepsi's early slogan 'Come Alive with the Pepsi Generation' being rendered into Mandarin in a way that implied bringing ancestors back from the dead. But it's not just translation failures: McDonald's runs broadly consistent global advertising while quietly adapting its menu and its emotional tone for each market, because what resonates as comforting or aspirational varies enormously. The underlying tension is between economies of scale from a standardised global campaign and the loss of local resonance when you don't adapt. Most brands with genuine global reach compromise: a global brand identity with localised execution.

commonMistakes: Framing the problem purely as a translation issue — it's much broader than words
No real example to ground the argument
Missing the strategic tension between standardisation and localisation
followUps: Which approach do you think is harder to get right — standardising globally or adapting locally — and why?

question: What do you understand by the term 'customer journey', and why does it matter for marketing strategy?
lookingFor: A clear grasp of the customer journey as a multi-stage process — from awareness through purchase and beyond — and an understanding of why different marketing activities need to serve different stages.

bullets: Define the customer journey as the sequence of stages a customer moves through from problem awareness to purchase and post-purchase
Name at least three stages — awareness, consideration, decision, loyalty or advocacy
Explain why mapping it matters: different messages, channels, and content types fit different stages
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The customer journey maps the stages a person moves through from first realising they have a need to making a purchase decision and beyond into post-purchase experience. A common framing runs from awareness — they discover your brand exists — through consideration, where they compare options, into decision, and then into loyalty and advocacy if the experience was good. Why it matters is that the right marketing intervention at the wrong stage often fails entirely. A hard sales message to someone who has never heard of your product rarely converts; brand advertising to someone who is ready to buy and just needs a price comparison is inefficient. Mapping the journey lets you design the right content and channel for each stage — broad reach advertising for awareness, detailed comparison content for consideration, clear calls to action for decision. The map also shows you where you're losing people, which is often more useful than knowing where you're winning them.

commonMistakes: Treating the journey as a funnel that ends at purchase and ignoring post-purchase
No connection to why different stages require different marketing approaches
Describing the journey abstractly with no practical implication
followUps: Where in the customer journey do you think most brands make their biggest mistakes, and why?

question: Tell us about a marketing campaign or brand decision you think went badly wrong, and explain why.
lookingFor: Sharp critical thinking applied to a real case — the candidate should identify the specific strategic or creative failure and draw a principle from it, not just recount what happened.

bullets: Name a real campaign or brand decision and give enough context for the panel to follow
Identify the specific marketing failure — misjudged audience, missed cultural context, wrong channel, misaligned brand values
Draw a principle from it that generalises beyond the single case
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Pepsi's 2017 Kendall Jenner ad is one I've thought about carefully because the failure is structurally interesting. The ad depicted a protest de-escalated when Jenner handed a police officer a Pepsi — it borrowed the visual language of Black Lives Matter while removing all the stakes and discomfort that made those images powerful. The backlash was immediate and severe. The failure wasn't creative incompetence; the production was glossy and professional. The failure was that the brand grafted itself onto a genuine social movement to borrow meaning it hadn't earned, and in doing so made light of the very things protesters were risking. The principle I take from it is that cultural appropriation in marketing — using the aesthetics of a movement without engaging with its substance — tends to fail badly precisely because the audience the brand is trying to impress is also the audience most likely to recognise what's been stripped out.

commonMistakes: Choosing a minor campaign error rather than a substantive strategic failure
Recounting what happened without analysing why it failed
Drawing no generalised lesson from the case
followUps: What should Pepsi have done differently — is there a version of that campaign that could have worked?

question: Should governments regulate advertising more heavily than they do? Make an argument and defend it.
lookingFor: Structured argument on a contested policy question — the candidate should form a view, support it with reasoning and evidence, and engage with the strongest counterargument rather than sitting on the fence.

bullets: State a clear position at the outset — yes, no, or in specific categories, with a reason
Support the position with at least two substantive arguments
Engage with the strongest objection to your position before concluding
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My view is that tighter regulation is justified in specific categories — particularly advertising aimed at children and advertising for products where the consumption harm is well-evidenced, like ultra-processed food and gambling. The argument for intervention is that the market alone doesn't internalise the social cost: an advertiser selling high-sugar products to children captures the revenue while healthcare systems absorb the long-run cost. That's a classic market failure and a legitimate case for regulation. The strongest counterargument is that regulation restricts information flow and that a better-informed consumer is a freer one — and I take that seriously in categories where harm is unclear. But where the harm is well-evidenced and the target audience has limited capacity to evaluate claims — children especially — I think restricting commercial speech is proportionate. The junk food advertising watershed in the UK is a reasonable model: it doesn't ban the advertising, it restricts the time and context of exposure.

commonMistakes: Refusing to take a position and presenting 'both sides' without commitment
A position with no substantive reasoning — 'yes because advertising is harmful'
No engagement with the strongest counterargument
followUps: Does your argument change if the product being advertised is alcohol rather than food?

question: Many brands now claim to have a social purpose beyond profit. Do you think purpose-led marketing is a genuine shift or mainly a commercial strategy?
lookingFor: Critical thinking about a significant current trend — the candidate should resist both naive acceptance and blanket cynicism, and show they can evaluate the evidence for whether purpose drives or merely decorates commercial strategy.

bullets: Acknowledge that purpose-led marketing has clear commercial logic — consumers and employees respond to it
Show scepticism: purpose can be deployed superficially as positioning without changing underlying practice
Land on a view about when purpose is genuine versus when it is performance
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I think it's genuinely both, and that's what makes it complicated. There is real evidence that purpose resonates commercially: consumers — particularly younger ones — say they prefer to buy from brands with values that align with theirs, and employee recruitment and retention is measurably easier for purpose-led businesses. So even a purely strategic leader has an incentive to invest in purpose. But that commercial incentive is also what makes it easy to perform purpose without practising it — the Dove Real Beauty example is instructive again, because the campaign values and Unilever's other brand practices sat in visible tension. I think the honest test is whether the purpose imposes any genuine cost or constraint on the company's decisions. A bank that says it cares about financial inclusion but continues to close branches in low-income areas hasn't changed anything; a business that turns down revenue-generating clients because of values conflicts is demonstrating something real. Without that test, purpose is largely aesthetic.

commonMistakes: Accepting all purpose-led marketing at face value
Dismissing it entirely as cynical with no acknowledgment of genuine examples
No concrete test for distinguishing genuine from performative purpose
followUps: Can you name a brand you believe has genuine purpose, and explain what evidence you base that on?

question: How do you think artificial intelligence will change marketing over the next decade, and what should marketers understand about that shift?
lookingFor: Thoughtful, grounded thinking about a genuine transformation — the candidate should go beyond 'AI will do everything faster' and show they understand both the opportunity and the things AI is structurally limited at.

bullets: Name at least two concrete ways AI is already changing or will change marketing practice — personalisation, content generation, media buying, data analysis
Identify at least one genuine limitation of AI in a marketing context
Say what this means for what marketers should focus on learning
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: AI is already transforming the parts of marketing that are high-volume, pattern-based, and rules-driven: programmatic media buying, personalisation at scale, A/B testing, and increasingly content generation at speed. Over the next decade I think the bigger shift will be in audience understanding — AI that analyses vast behavioural datasets to identify insights a human analyst would miss. The limitation that interests me most is strategic and cultural judgment. AI is trained on what has worked historically, which means it tends to optimise within established patterns rather than challenge them. The Dove Real Beauty campaign worked because someone decided to contradict the industry's own norms — that kind of strategic insight, grounded in genuine human understanding, is harder to generate from pattern-matching. I think the marketers who thrive will be those who use AI to handle execution and analysis, freeing themselves to focus on the strategic and creative questions that require judgment, empathy, and cultural fluency that AI doesn't yet replicate well.

commonMistakes: Treating AI as an unambiguous improvement in all areas of marketing
No specific examples of what AI actually does or will do
Missing the implication for what human marketers should focus on developing
followUps: Which skill do you think will be most valuable for a marketer to have in a world where AI handles a lot of execution?
